core.action: Standardize on a single market impact model across each trading environments, ensuring consistent execution cost estimates and policy optimization — either square-root law (BD-107 with Y=0.6) or Almgren-Chriss decomposition (BD-031 with gamma coefficient)

consequence.description: Inconsistent market impact models across environments cause backtest-live discrepancy where strategies optimized under one model produce different execution costs under another, making performance comparisons unreliable

evidence_refs.summary: 15405db Derived from BD: BD-149

core.when: When implementing market impact modeling in backtesting
core.action: Use square root market impact model (Almgren-Chriss style) — linear models underestimate large order costs; cubic models overpenalize and discourage legitimate trades

consequence.description: Using linear impact model causes execution cost underestimation for large orders, leading to backtest results that are optimistic compared to live trading where large orders face significant market impact

evidence_refs.summary: 15405db Derived from BD: BD-031

core.when: When configuring market impact model parameters for stock backtesting
core.action: Verify that eta=0.1 permanent impact coefficient matches the stock's liquidity profile — higher values (0.5) indicate illiquid stocks; lower values (0.01) are for highly liquid assets

consequence.description: Using default eta=0.1 for illiquid stocks underestimates permanent price impact by 80%, causing backtest returns to exceed what would occur in live trading; for highly liquid stocks, it overestimates impact by 10x, causing strategies to undertrade

evidence_refs.summary: 15405db Derived from BD: BD-032

core.when: When adjusting Almgren-Chriss execution model parameters
core.action: Recalibrate each coupled parameters together when changing any single parameter — volatility=0.12 (BD-106) for timing risk interacts with square-root impact coefficient Y=0.6 (BD-107) to determine optimal execution trajectories

consequence.description: Changing one parameter without recalibrating others creates misaligned risk-cost tradeoffs, leading to either excessive market impact costs or uncompensated timing risk in execution strategies

core.action: Use lambda=0.95 for advantage estimation in PPO — this specific value balances bias-variance tradeoff, reducing variance while maintaining controlled bias for stable policy gradient learning

core.action: Use epsilon clip of 0.2 to constrain policy ratio to [0.8, 1.2] range — this prevents catastrophically destructive policy updates while allowing sufficient learning speed

core.when: When configuring episode termination logic in training environments
core.action: Verify the 10% portfolio threshold matches the strategy's acceptable loss limits — higher threshold (50%) triggers too early and wastes learning opportunities; lower threshold (1%) risks numerical instability with near-zero equity values

consequence.description: An inappropriate termination threshold causes episodes to either end prematurely before meaningful learning occurs, or continue with numerically unstable positions that corrupt training gradients and degrade model quality

core.when: When modeling transaction costs in backtesting
core.action: Verify that percentage-based transaction costs match actual exchange fee structures — percentage costs scale with trade size correctly, but may not capture fixed exchange fees for small trades or tiered fee structures; fixed costs distort small trades and tiered costs add complexity

consequence.description: Incorrect cost modeling causes systematic over- or under-estimation of strategy profitability; small trades appear more profitable than they would be in live trading due to unmodeled fixed fees, while large trades may appear less profitable due to percentage caps

core.action: Verify slippage assumptions match the target futures market — slippage=0 provides a clean baseline but underestimates real execution costs; typical slippage (0.01%) should be added for realistic backtests; negative slippage is unrealistic

consequence.description: Zero slippage baseline causes backtested results to appear more profitable than live trading would deliver, as actual fills occur at slightly worse prices than the quoted price; this creates false expectations and live trading disappointment
